title: On symplectomorphism groups of some Milnor fibres
speaker: Ailsa Keating
tags: Homological Mirror Symmetry: Methods and Structures

proof_stages.label: Milnor-fibre monodromy representation
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the main problem as understanding the representation from the fundamental group of the complement of the discriminant in the miniversal deformation to the compactly supported symplectic mapping class group of the Milnor fibre, generated geometrically by Dehn twists in vanishing cycles.

proof_stages.label: A'Campo real-morsification model
proof_stages.summary: For two-variable singularities, possibly after stabilization, a good real morsification is used to read off all critical points and to build the Milnor fibre by cylinders, half-twisted strips, and vanishing cycles associated to crossings and bounded regions.

proof_stages.label: Diagrammatic Fukaya-category encoding
proof_stages.summary: The geometric vanishing-cycle configuration is compressed to a planar diagram whose vertices are vanishing cycles, edges give rank-one morphisms, and oriented triangles give m_2 products; after sufficient stabilization the full subcategory on the vanishing cycles is described by a formal trivial-extension category.

proof_stages.label: Diagram group with triangle relations
proof_stages.summary: From the diagram D_f the speaker defines a group G(D_f) with Dehn-twist-like generators, commuting and braid relations for nonedges and edges, and an extra four-term relation for each oriented triangle, justified by the local triangle product configuration.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with the discriminant-complement group
proof_stages.summary: The group G(D_f) is asserted to be independent of choices of real morsification, to map onto pi_1 of the discriminant complement, and to agree with it in all known cases such as ADE and Brieskorn-Pham singularities.

proof_stages.label: Stabilization and kernel comparison
proof_stages.summary: After explaining that stabilization can remove low-dimensional relations coming from nonformal A-infinity products, the speaker states the converse kernel theorem: any relation killed in a nontrivially stabilized symplectic mapping-class representation is already killed in the lowest-dimensional two-variable case.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and intersection-theoretic proof sketch
proof_stages.summary: The kernel theorem yields faithful stabilized actions in cases such as A_n and D_n from known surface injectivity, and its proof is sketched via intersection-number inequalities for vanishing cycles meeting only zero or one times, plus an enlargement of the Weinstein domain to rule out boundary twists.

examples.name: The x^4+y^4 morsification
examples.note: The polynomial x^4+y^4, with Milnor number 9, is used as a worked singularity whose real morsification makes A'Campo's surface, vanishing cycles, and diagrammatic Fukaya-category data concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes the Milnor number as 9, analyzes the real morsification diagram to count saddles and regions, and runs A’Campo’s construction to build the surface and vanishing cycles.

passes.reason: It is used as a concrete Brieskorn-Pham morsification to illustrate A’Campo’s algorithm, not as a structural exception or failure case.

examples.name: The E6 singularity diagrams
examples.note: The E6 singularity, identified as x^3+y^4, illustrates that different real morsifications can produce different-looking diagrams but the same diagram group.
examples.engagement_reason: He displays/points to two possible E6 diagrams and says they give the same associated group, but does not derive or check this on the diagrams.

passes.reason: E6 is one of the exceptional ADE types, and the diagrams are used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Brieskorn-Pham grid diagrams x^p+y^q
examples.note: The family x^p+y^q is used to show the checkered planar diagrams that arise in a known class where the diagram group presentation matches the discriminant-complement fundamental group.
examples.engagement_reason: He says Brieskorn–Pham singularities x^p+y^q have checkered grid-like diagrams with alternating triangle signs, without working through the construction or relations.

passes.reason: The x^p+y^q Brieskorn-Pham diagrams form a systematic infinite family with a regular checkered grid structure.

examples.name: The unstabilized E6 kernel relation alpha
examples.note: A Wajnryb-type relation for the surface E6 Milnor fibre is presented as the phenomenon showing that low-dimensional mapping-class relations can disappear after stabilization, motivating the formal-category explanation.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the existence of an unstabilized E6 kernel class alpha and facts about what happens after stabilization, but does not compute or exhibit the relation.

passes.reason: The class alpha is presented as a kernel element showing non-faithfulness in the unstabilized E6 action.

examples.name: A_n and D_n faithful actions
examples.note: The A_n and D_n cases illustrate how the kernel-comparison theorem turns known injectivity for the Riemann-surface Milnor fibre into faithful stabilized symplectic mapping-class actions.
examples.engagement_reason: After stating faithful A_n and D_n actions, he sketches the proof using the 0/1 intersection property, Dehn-twist products, parity/equality of intersections, and boundary-twist avoidance.

passes.reason: The A_n and D_n cases are infinite-family positive faithful-action results, not exceptions or counterexamples.
The A_n and D_n actions are faithful, so their representations have the minimal possible kernel, and are used positively.
The A_n and D_n cases are ordinary infinite-family Dynkin types, cited as positive faithful actions rather than counterexamples.
The A_n and D_n actions are standard infinite-family cases used as positive faithful actions, not as failures.
